title: Testing your Python code with pytest
text:
Imagine you write a function to convert temperatures from Celsius to Fahrenheit. It seems to work when you try a couple of values by hand, so you move on. Two weeks later, a colleague tells you the weather app you both built is showing wrong temperatures in extreme cold. The bug was there all along — you just never checked that edge case.
Tests are small pieces of code that automatically check whether your functions behave as expected. Think of a function as a contract, the function is supposed to carry out a particular task and the test assures you that it is really doing what is supposed to do. They let you:

Make sure that your functions are doing what they are supposed to do.
Catch bugs early.
Safely change or improve your code, knowing the tests will alert you if something breaks.

Good developers write tests alongside their code, not as an afterthought.

title: Testing your Python code with pytest
text:

Two weeks later, a colleague tells you the weather app you both built is showing wrong temperatures in extreme cold. The bug was there all along — you just never checked that edge case.
Tests are small pieces of code that automatically check whether your functions behave as expected. Think of a function as a contract, the function is supposed to carry out a particular task and the test assures you that it is really doing what is supposed to do. They let you:

title: Testing your Python code with pytest
text:
Setting up pytest
pytest is a popular Python testing library. It is not part of the standard library, so you need to install it once:
uv add pytest
To verify the installation worked:
uv run pytest --version
You should see something like pytest 8.x.x.

title: Testing your Python code with pytest
text:
Your first test
Let’s start with the simplest possible example: a function that adds two numbers.






This looks correct, but running it by hand every time we change the code is tedious and error-prone. Let’s write a test instead. Save this code in a file named add.py
In pytest, a test is just a regular Python function whose name starts with test_. Inside it, we use assert to check that something is true.
Here is what the test file would look like. Save it as test_math.py:

# test_add.py


def test_add_positive_numbers():
    assert add(2, 3) == 5


def test_add_negative_numbers():
    assert add(-1, -1) == -2


def test_add_zero():
    assert add(0, 5) == 5

To run the tests, open a terminal in the same folder and type:
pytest test_add.py
You will see output like this:
============================= test session starts ==============================
collected 3 items

test_math.py ...                                                         [100%]

============================== 3 passed in 0.01s ===============================
Each dot (.) represents one test that passed. If a test fails, you get an F and a detailed explanation of what went wrong.

title: Testing your Python code with pytest
text:
What does assert do?
assert checks that a condition is True. If it is not, it raises an AssertionError and pytest marks that test as failed.












When pytest runs a test and an assert fails, it prints a clear message showing you the expected value versus what your function actually returned.

title: Testing your Python code with pytest
text:
How pytest finds your tests
pytest automatically discovers tests by looking for:

Files whose names start with test_ or end with _test.py.
Functions inside those files whose names start with test_.

This means you don’t have to register your tests anywhere — just follow the naming convention and run pytest.

title: Testing your Python code with pytest

title: Testing your Python code with pytest
text:
Organising your test files
As your project grows, you will have many test files. A typical structure looks like this:
my_project/
│
├── conversions.py        # your code
├── math_utils.py         # more code
│
└── tests/
    ├── test_conversions.py
    └── test_math_utils.py
To run all tests at once, just run pytest (with no arguments) from the project root. It will find every test_ file automatically.

title: Testing your Python code with pytest
text:
Writing tests alongside your code
A healthy habit is to write tests as you write the code, not after. Here is a typical workflow:

Write a small function.
Write one or two tests for it.
Run pytest. Make sure the tests pass.
Add more tests for edge cases.
Run pytest again.

This way you build confidence in your code as you go, and bugs are caught immediately rather than weeks later.

title: Testing your Python code with pytest
text:
Python Testing book
If you want to know more about pytest you can take a look at this excellent book: Python Testing with pytest

title: Testing your Python code with pytest
text:
Exercises
Now it is your turn. For each exercise, write both the function and the tests for it in a .py file and run them with pytest.






NoteExercise 1 — Kilometre to mile converter



Write a function km_to_miles(km) that converts kilometres to miles (1 km = 0.621371 miles).
Then write tests for:

0 km → 0 miles
1 km → approximately 0.621371 miles
10 km → approximately 6.21371 miles
100 km → approximately 62.1371 miles

Remember to use pytest.approx for the floating-point comparisons.








NoteExercise 2 — Seconds converter



Write a function seconds_to_hms(total_seconds) that receives a number of seconds and returns a tuple (hours, minutes, seconds).
For example:

seconds_to_hms(3661) → (1, 1, 1) (1 hour, 1 minute, 1 second)
seconds_to_hms(0) → (0, 0, 0)
seconds_to_hms(60) → (0, 1, 0)
seconds_to_hms(3600) → (1, 0, 0)

Write at least five tests.








NoteExercise 3 — Safe integer parser



Write a function safe_parse_int(text) that:

Returns the integer value if text is a valid integer string (e.g. "42" → 42).
Raises a ValueError with a helpful message if text cannot be converted.

Write tests for valid inputs like "0", "100", "-5", and tests using pytest.raises for invalid inputs like "abc", "3.14", and "".








NoteExercise 4 — List statistics



Write three functions:

my_max(numbers) — returns the largest number in the list.
my_min(numbers) — returns the smallest number in the list.
my_average(numbers) — returns the average of the list.

All three should raise a ValueError when given an empty list.
Write tests for each function, including tests that verify the ValueError is raised for an empty list.
Challenge: Can you write a test that checks my_average returns the correct result for a list with a single element?








NoteExercise 5 — Word counter



Write a function count_words(sentence) that counts the number of words in a string. A word is any sequence of characters separated by spaces.

count_words("hello world") → 2
count_words("one") → 1
count_words("") → 0
count_words("  ") → 0 (a string of spaces has no words)

Write tests for all four cases above, plus at least two more that you think of yourself.
